title: Dodonaea viscosa subsp. elaeagnoides
what_description: Dodonaea viscosa subsp. elaeagnoides is a plant species within the family Sapindaceae, commonly known as the soapberry family. It is a shrub or small tree that grows up to 6 meters in height, with a greyish-brown bark, smooth or slightly fissured. The leaves are alternate, elliptical, or ovate in shape, 4-14 cm long, and 2-8 cm wide, with a distinctive midrib and 5-11 pairs of lateral veins. The inflorescence is a panicle or a corymb, with small, greenish-yellow flowers that are unisexual and dioecious. The fruit is a drupe, 7-10 mm long, with a single seed surrounded by a fleshy, edible pulp.
ailments_description: The most common ailments you can heal with Dodonaea viscosa subsp. elaeagnoides are inflammation, diabetes, fever reduction and gastrointestinal issues due to its anti-inflammatory and antidiabetic properties. This plant has been proven to reduce fungal infections and skin irritation through its antifungal and antimicrobial activities. Additionally, Dodonaea viscosa subsp. elaeagnoides has been shown to expedite wound healing, alleviate respiratory issues, and manage rheumatism and arthritis through its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties. Its extracts have also been found to treat insect bites, skin infections, and cough by reducing inflammation and fighting pathogens. Furthermore, Dodonaea viscosa subsp. elaeagnoides has been used to treat hypertension, wound, digestive issues, diarrhea, anxiety, and infections due to its vasodilatory and antioxidant effects.
properties_description: The main therapeutic properties of Dodonaea viscosa subsp. elaeagnoides are its antioxidant effects, which are attributed to the presence of flavonoids and phenolic acids that help protect cells from oxidative damage. The plant's antimicrobial activity, which is particularly effective against fungi, is due to the bioactive compounds saponins and volatile oils that disrupt microbial cell membranes. Its anti-inflammatory properties are attributed to the presence of flavonoids and terpenoids that inhibit the production of pro-inflammatory enzymes and cytokines, thereby reducing inflammation and alleviating conditions such as arthritis. The plant's antibacterial properties are also attributed to the presence of saponins and phenolic acids that inhibit the growth of bacteria and prevent the formation of biofilms. Additionally, the plant's analgesic properties are attributed to the presence of flavonoids and terpenoids that inhibit the production of pain-causing chemicals in the body, providing relief from pain and discomfort.
constituents_description: The most important healing constituents of Dodonaea viscosa subsp. elaeagnoides are flavonoids, including kaempferol, quercetin, and myricetin, which have been shown to exhibit antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and antimicrobial activities. Phenolic acids, such as ferulic and sinapic acid, are also present and have been found to have antimicrobial and anti-inflammatory properties. Tannins, a class of polyphenolic compounds, have been isolated from the plant and have been reported to have antimicrobial and astringent activities. Coumarins, including scopoletin and umbelliferone, have been identified as constituents of Dodonaea viscosa subsp. elaeagnoides and have been found to have anti-inflammatory and anticoagulant activities. Additionally, cardiac glycosides, such as dodonaeine, have been isolated from the plant and have been reported to have cardiotonic and diuretic activities.
preparations_description: The main herbal preparations of Dodonaea viscosa subsp. elaeagnoides are made from its leaves and bark, which are rich in flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids. Infusion is prepared by steeping dried leaves in hot water to treat respiratory issues and fever, while decoction involves boiling the bark in water to use as a treatment for skin conditions and wounds. Tincture is made by extracting the active compounds from the leaves and bark with a solvent, such as ethanol, to create a medicinal liquid for internal use. Salve is prepared by infusing the leaves in oil to create a topical treatment for skin irritations and wounds, and tea is made by infusing the leaves in hot water to treat digestive issues and fever. These herbal preparations have been traditionally used in Australian Aboriginal medicine to treat various health conditions.
side_effects_description: The possible side effects of using Dodonaea viscosa subsp. elaeagnoides improperly include experiencing nausea, which can be caused by the plant's toxic compounds such as dodonone and isododone, that can irritate the digestive system. Developing skin irritation is also a possible side effect due to the plant's sap, which contains allergens that can cause skin inflammation and redness. Experiencing allergic reactions is another potential side effect, as the plant's compounds can trigger an immune response in some individuals, leading to symptoms such as hives, itching, and swelling. Headaches can also occur due to the plant's volatile oils, which can cause irritation to the nervous system and lead to headaches and migraines. These side effects can be exacerbated by improper use, such as ingesting the plant or using it in excessive amounts.
